The plane was flying over Paris one hour ago. Last night, the sun was setting over the ocean. Notice that the events described by these examples have already occurred and that, when the events occurred, they were ongoing, or continuous. 💡MUST KNOW: The past continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that was ongoing but has ended.

item.title: 6.10.3 The Future Continuous Tense
item.description: Lesson Content The future continuous tense is used to describe an action or event that will be ongoing but has not yet begun; the action or event will occur in the future. The children will be playing outside tomorrow. The plane will be flying over Madrid in about five hours. Astronomers will be studying the comet as it passes through the inner solar system. Notice that the events described by these examples will be ongoing at some point in the future.

item.title: 6.10.4 The Perfect Continuous Tenses
item.description: Lesson Content Just as the simple present, simple past, and simple future tenses have continuous tense counterparts, the perfect tenses – the present perfect, past perfect, and future perfect – have continuous counterparts. To compare the present perfect tense and the present perfect continuous tense, consider the following examples: That eagle has flown overhead many times. That eagle has been flying overhead for the last twenty minutes. In the first example, the present perfect is used to convey that, over a period of time extending from the past to the present, the eagle has flown overhead on multiple occasions.

item.title: 6.10.5 Determining Whether to Use a Non-Continuous or Continuous Tense
item.description: Lesson Content Correctly answering a Sentence Correction question can require determining whether using a non-continuous tense or using a continuous tense makes more sense. When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best. When sentences do not involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use non-continuous tenses are the best. 💡MUST KNOW: When sentences involve reasons to highlight the ongoing nature of events, versions that use continuous tenses are the best.

item.title: 6.11 Verb Mood
item.description: Lesson Content Some Sentence Correction questions will test our skill in assessing use of verb mood. 💡MUST KNOW: Whereas verb tense lets us place events in time, verb mood conveys the attitude with which a statement is made. Let’s begin with the indicative verb mood.

item.title: 6.12 Indicative Mood
item.description: Lesson Content The indicative mood is the most commonly used verb mood and is used for stating facts, assertions, and observations. The flowers smell good. Tomorrow, we will go to the beach. It may be necessary to change the design of the outer shell. The panel concluded that the bank should stop financing oil projects completely by 2008. Once ranging as far east as Arkansas and Louisiana, ocelots are currently found only in extreme southern Texas and northeastern Mexico.

item.title: 6.13 The Subjunctive Mood: Hypothetical Situations
item.description: Lesson Content The subjunctive mood is used for statements that lack certainty, such as those that describe hypothetical or conditional situations, dreams, wishes, desires, or doubts. In a sentence that describes a hypothetical situation, the form of “to be” that should be used is the verb “were,” never the verb “was.” At first, this usage will probably sound incorrect to you. 💡MUST KNOW: Sentences that describe hypothetical situations must use the subjunctive mood.

item.title: 6.14 The Subjunctive Mood is Not Used in Sentences that Express Uncertainty
item.description: Lesson Content There is a major difference between a hypothetical situation and a situation in which the outcome is real, yet the speaker is unsure of that outcome. 💡MUST KNOW: There is a major difference between a hypothetical situation and a situation in which the outcome is real, yet the speaker is unsure of that outcome. Consider the following example sentence: If this painting was painted by Rembrandt, then it is an amazing find.

item.title: 6.15 Use of the Subjunctive Mood in Command Situations
item.description: Lesson Content Along with hypothetical situations, certain types of “command situations” require the use of the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the “command subjunctive.” In these situations, we’ll see a request, suggestion, demand, or command word followed by the word “that.” 💡MUST KNOW: Request, suggestion, demand, or command words followed by the word “that” require the subjunctive mood, which, in these situations, is referred to as the command subjunctive.

item.title: 6.16 The Conditional Mood
item.description: Lesson Content Like the subjunctive mood, the conditional mood is used in sentences about conditions and results that are purely hypothetical. 💡MUST KNOW: The conditional mood is used in sentences about conditions and results that are purely hypothetical. The conditional mood can be formed by combining “would” or “could” with a finite verb. Consider the following examples, each of which describes a purely hypothetical situation: If I knew how to speak French, I would move to Paris.

item.title: 6.17 The Conditional Mood Is Not Used in Sentences that Are Not Purely Hypothetical
item.description: Lesson Content Remember, the conditional mood is used in sentences describing purely hypothetical things. 💡MUST KNOW: The conditional mood is used in sentences describing purely hypothetical things. It is not used in situations that could actually be true. For review, consider the following sentence versions: Incorrect: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it will be very popular. Incorrect: If the movie were as good as the trailer, it should be very popular.

item.title: 6.18 Verb Voice: Active Voice vs. Passive Voice
item.description: Lesson Content In addition to using variations in verb tense and verb mood to test our skill in assessing the effectiveness of sentences, SC questions use verb voice. There are two verb voices, active voice and passive voice. Active voice is the more commonly used verb voice. 💡MUST KNOW: When the subject of a clause performs the action, the corresponding verb is in the active voice. Consider the sentence below:

item.title: 6.18.1 Deciding Between Answer Choices That Use the Active Voice or Passive Voice
item.description: Lesson Content Most of the time, the active voice is preferred to the passive voice, because in most cases, sentences are more concise and convey their meanings more clearly when they are written in active voice than when they are written in passive voice. 💡MUST KNOW: Most of the time, the active voice is preferred to the passive voice. For example, notice that the active voice version of the two sentences below does a much better job of conveying what occurred than the convoluted and awkward passive voice version does.

item.title: 6.18.2 Don’t Eliminate a Sentence Version Just Because its Written in the Passive Voice
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, we know well that we’ll encounter situations in which the correct answers are written in the passive voice. 💡MUST KNOW: In SC questions, we will encounter situations in which the correct answers are written in the passive voice, so don’t rule out a sentence simply because it is written in the passive voice. In fact, one way in which a question writer can make the incorrectness or correctness of answer choices less obvious is to use the active voice in incorrect choices and the passive voice in the correct answer to a question, thus tempting test-takers to choose the more conventional sounding, active voice incorrect choice rather than the less conventional sounding, passive voice correct answer.

item.title: 6.18.3 In Questions Testing Verb Voice, it’s Often Smart to Seek Other Errors Too.
item.description: Lesson Content One theme that we’ve discussed often is how we’ll score the most points on SC questions when we seek out the most significant mistakes we can find and then eliminate the sentence versions containing them. Strategic Reminder: We’ll score the most points on SC questions when we seek out the most significant mistakes we can find and then eliminate the sentence versions containing them. This theme is often underscored in questions involving verb voice because there may be times when we’re unsure whether the use of one of the two verb voices alone is sufficient to make a SC answer choice clearly incorrect.

item.title: 7.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Parallelism is all around us, and it lends clarity and forcefulness to writing. “Early to bed, early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise.” In the above example, the three adjectives “healthy,” “wealthy,” and “wise” are parallel. “Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears; I come to bury Caesar, not to praise him.” In this second example, a parallel structure is made up of two verbs in infinitive form (“to + verb”).

item.title: 7.2 There are Common Triggers that Alert Us to Check for Proper Parallelism
item.description: Lesson Content People often wonder when to check for parallelism. 💡MUST KNOW: There are firmly established triggers that alert us to check for proper parallelism. Each time we encounter one of these triggers in a sentence, we check to be sure that the elements in that sentence are parallel. In this chapter, we’ll cover each of these parallelism triggers, such as the FANBOYS conjunctions, correlative conjunctions, including “not only … but also,” and items found in a list.

item.title: 7.3 Parallelism Has Little to Do with How a Sentence Sounds to Your Ear
item.description: Lesson Content A common theme we’ll encounter is that parallelism has little to do with how a sentence sounds. We’ll encounter many sentences that “sound” parallel, yet are not. 💡MUST KNOW: Parallelism has little to do with how a sentence sounds. Instead, parallelism has everything to do with how a sentence is structured. Don’t rely on your “ear”! Let’s now discuss a concept that is foundational in understanding parallelism: structures built around coordinating conjunctions, also known as FANBOYS, must be parallel.

item.title: 7.4 FANBOYS Always Alert Us to Check for Proper Parallelism
item.description: Lesson Content We already know that the coordinating conjunctions are “for,” “and,” “nor,” “but,” “or,” “yet,” and “so.” We use the mnemonic device FANBOYS to remember them. 💡MUST KNOW: The coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS): For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So. These FANBOYS conjunctions are always parallelism markers, and a structure built around one of the FANBOYS must be parallel. In other words, any time we encounter one of these FANBOYS conjunctions, we must be vigilant in ensuring that parallelism is properly maintained in the sentence in which it’s found.

item.title: 7.4.1 How to Determine Whether a Structure Built Around a FANBOYS Is Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content There are many methods that can be used to determine whether structures in a sentence are written with proper parallelism, but many of these are either just too grammatical or too complicated to work well on test day. Instead, we’re going to use a method that is based on logic and is easy to use on test day. To determine whether a structure built around a FANBOYS is parallel:

item.title: 7.5 Structures Built Around FANBOYS are Essentially Lists of Two Items
item.description: Lesson Content Without realizing it, you’ve been not only learning about parallelism in structures built across FANBOYS but also learning about parallelism in lists, which frequently appear in SC questions. More specifically, you’ve been learning about parallelism in lists of two items because the structures built across FANBOYS that we have been discussing are essentially two-item lists. 💡MUST KNOW: Structures built around FANBOYS conjunctions are two-element lists. These lists must maintain proper parallel structure.

item.title: 7.6 Parallelism in Lists
item.description: Lesson Content A list consists of two or more items in a series, and the items in a list are generally separated by commas or coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS). The items in a list must be parallel. 💡MUST KNOW: A list consists of two or more items in a series, often separated by commas. The items in a list must be parallel. The good news is that, because you already know a lot about checking for parallelism across the FANBOYS conjunctions, the skills needed for checking for parallelism in a list will be much easier to master.

item.title: 7.6.1 Carry Over in Lists
item.description: Lesson Content “Carrying over” simply refers to the concept that the sentence structures that introduce a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten. 💡MUST KNOW: “Carrying over” refers to the concept that certain elements of a structure involving a list are understood to be repeated throughout the list, although these elements are not explicitly rewritten. You saw this concept (although we didn’t use the term carrying over) a short while ago in the sections on parallelism across FANBOYS.

item.title: 7.6.2 How to Determine Whether the Items in a List are Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content To determine whether the items in a list are parallel, we’ll follow the following steps: Step1: Notice the list in the sentence. Lists are always parallelism triggers. Please leave a message including your name, your number, and explain why you called. Step 2: Identify the elements of the list. Our next move in analyzing a sentence for proper parallelism in a list is to identify the elements of the list.

item.title: 7.6.3 Repeating Elements Essential to What Introduces a List Cannot Be Haphazardly Placed in a List
item.description: Lesson Content From our discussion of carrying over, we know that we need not explicitly write a word such as “to” before every element in a list in a sentence like the following: The members of the committee decided to reduce marketing expenditures, to increase headcount significantly, and to change the schedule. In other words, one “to” in front of the first element of the list is perfect, as you’ve seen in sentences like the following:

item.title: 7.6.4 Sometimes a Word in a List Must Be Repeated For Clarity
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to be clear, it’s necessary to repeat certain words by placing them before every item in the list. Let’s start with prepositions.

item.title: 7.6.5 Sometimes Prepositions Must be Repeated for a List to Clearly Convey a Logical Meaning
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a preposition, such as “for,” “of,” “to,” “in,” “by,” or “because of” by placing the preposition before every item in the list. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a preposition, such as “for,” “of,” “to,” “in,” “by,” or “because of” by placing the preposition before every item in the list.

item.title: 7.6.6 Sometimes “That” Must be Repeated to Make a List Clear
item.description: Lesson Content Similar to what we saw with repeated prepositions, sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a “that” by placing it before every item in the list. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, in order for a sentence including a list to clearly convey a logical meaning, it’s necessary to repeat a “that” by placing it before every item in the list.

item.title: 7.6.7 Nested Lists
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes we’ll encounter a list within a list. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes we’ll encounter a list within a list. We call these structures nested lists. In other words, a nested list is a list within one of the elements of another list. Consider the following example sentence: Please leave your name and your number and explain why you called. Notice that we have a two-part list. Please leave (your name and your number) and explain (why you called).

item.title: 7.6.8 Understanding the Carry Over Within a Nested List
item.description: Lesson Content We now know how to recognize a nested list, and we understand the importance of keeping the outer list and the nested list straight. Our next important concept to understand is what carries over in the nested list and in the outer list. Please leave your name and your number and explain why you called. We know that the outer list is the following. Please: leave (your name and your number)

item.title: 7.6.9 Correct Answers that Fix a Broken List by Changing It to a Nested List
item.description: Lesson Content As we know, it’s common to encounter SC questions in which the original sentence contains a broken list (a list constructed without proper parallelism). 💡MUST KNOW: It’s common to encounter SC questions in which the original sentence contains a broken list (a list constructed without proper parallelism). In these situations, it’s common to find that the correct answer fixes the broken list by changing it into a nested list.

item.title: 7.7 Correlative Conjunctions Must be Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content In Sentence Correction questions, we commonly encounter correlative conjunctions, the most important of which to learn are: not only … but also not only … but … as well not … but both … and either … or neither … nor 💡MUST KNOW: The most important correlative conjunctions to learn are “not only … but also,” “not only … but … as well,” “not … but,” “both … and,” “either … or,” “neither … nor.

item.title: 7.7.1 The Correlative Conjunction “Not Only … But Also”
item.description: Lesson Content One of the most commonly used correlative conjunctions is “not only … but also.” Likely, you’re used to seeing this conjunction in sentences. For example: Marty eats not only avocados but also bananas. Jeff not only visited Patagonia but also spent time on Easter Island. She wrote him not only a love letter but also a poem. Not only does Inder love studying for the GMAT, but also he loves writing his essays.

item.title: 7.7.2 Whatever Precedes the First Part of a “Not Only … But Also” Structure Carries Over to the Second Part.
item.description: Lesson Content Whatever precedes the first part of a “not only … but also” structure is understood to carry over to the second part of the structure, and thus does not have to be repeated in order for a complete, logical thought to be expressed. 💡MUST KNOW: Whatever precedes the first part of a “not only … but also” correlative structure is understood to carry over to, without being written in, the second part of the correlative structure.

item.title: 7.7.3 Whatever Appears After “Not Only” Does Not Carry Over to After “But Also.”
item.description: Lesson Content In the last section, we saw that an essential element that precedes “not only” is understood to carry over, without being written, to what comes after “but also.” In contrast, an element that comes directly after “not only” does not carry over to after “but also.” So, if any such element is necessary after “but also,” it must be repeated. 💡MUST KNOW: In a “not only … but also” structure, elements that follow “not only” are not understood to carry over to after “but also.

item.title: 7.7.4 The Placement of Words Between “But” and “Also” Can Result in an Illogical Sentence.
item.description: Lesson Content When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism. 💡MUST KNOW: When “not only … but also” is used, we must pay careful attention to any words in between “but” and “also,” because the placement of words between “but” and “also” can mess up the parallelism.

item.title: 7.7.5 Other Correlative Conjunctions
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, you’ve already learned a great deal about the correlative structure “not only … but also.” The good news, as promised, is that the patterns that apply to “not only … but also” apply almost identically to the other correlative conjunctions. Let’s continue by talking about carry over and correlative conjunctions.

item.title: 7.7.6 Carry-Over and other Correlative Conjunctions
item.description: Lesson Content As you saw with the correlative conjunction “not only … but also,” whatever essential element precedes “not only” is understood to carry over to the “but also” part of the construction. not only … but … as well not … but both … and either … or neither … nor For example, consider the following sentence: Incorrect: Each day, Sam is charged with either mopping the floors or he must take out the trash.

item.title: 7.8 Other Structures that Demand Parallelism
item.description: Lesson Content So far in this chapter, we’ve seen a number of situations in which parallelism must be maintained. X rather than Y mistake X for Y prefer X to Y range from X to Y more X than Y (in certain contexts) Consider the following example sentence: The team traveled by rail rather than flew to the tournament. Notice, what precedes “rather than,” “traveled,” is parallel to what follows “rather than,” “flew.

item.title: 7.9 Parallel Does Not Always Mean Exactly the Same.
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes in learning about parallelism, people come away with the idea that the elements of a parallel structure must always match each other closely. Let’s review some common situations in which elements that may not seem parallel are considered parallel, starting with the fact that verbs in different tenses can be perfectly parallel.

item.title: 7.9.1 Verbs That Are in Different Tenses Can be Perfectly Parallel.
item.description: Lesson Content Some people believe that verbs that are in different tenses are not parallel to each other. 💡MUST KNOW: Verbs that are in different tenses can be parallel. For example, consider the following sentence that has different tenses, past perfect and past continuous, in the same list: The ecologist said that genetically modified fish had found their way into the lake, had grown quickly, and were eating the other fish.

item.title: 7.9.2 Active Voice and Passive Voice in the Same List Can be Parallel
item.description: Lesson Content One might assume that it’s incorrect to have both active voice verbs and passive voice verbs in the same list. 💡MUST KNOW: There is nothing wrong with having active voice verbs and passive voice verbs together in a list. Consider the following example: Having hit her GMAT target, Aisha applied to her first-choice business school, was invited for an interview, and was accepted. As we already know well, each component of a list must work logically with the essential element or elements that introduce the list.

item.title: 7.9.3 Clauses in a Sentence Do Not Have to Be Structured in the Same Way to Be Parallel.
item.description: Lesson Content When considering parallelism of clauses in a sentence, there is one simple rule that we can follow: Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel. After all, clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs. 💡MUST KNOW: Clauses in a sentence do not have to be structured in the same way to be parallel because clauses are parallel to each other in that they all have subjects and verbs.

item.title: 7.10 When, other than in degree of parallelism, two versions of a sentence seem equally correct, choose the more parallel version.
item.description: Lesson Content We have discussed the fact that parallelism is largely based on logic and the fact that structures don’t have to match exactly in order to be acceptably parallel. At the same time, we have seen that some structures are more parallel than others. Rome is one of the few cities in the world where a great number of well-preserved ancient structures can be found and that has many examples of stunning Renaissance architecture.

item.title: 7.11 Gerunds and Infinitives – Special Cases
item.description: Lesson Content Gerunds, verb forms that end in –ing, such as “sailing” or “writing,” are essentially nouns, and infinitives, verbs preceded by “to,” such as “to sail” or “to write,” can also be used as nouns. However, despite this similarity between gerunds and infinitives, the convention followed by the GMAT is that gerunds and infinitives are not parallel. 💡MUST KNOW: The GMAT prefers versions that do not use gerunds and infinitives together in lists.

item.title: 8.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content It’s hard to get through the Verbal section of the GMAT without encountering Sentence Correction questions that involve comparisons. In a comparison, two or more things, ideas, concepts, entities, or people are compared. For example: John is like Melissa; they both love Caribbean beaches. The teeth of the gray wolf are similar in size to the teeth of the red wolf. Federico can run the 40-yard dash as quickly as Johanna can.

item.title: 8.2 Identifying Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content There are a number of signal words that can alert us that a comparison is being made. in the same way than at the same rate similar to correspondingly much as so whereas while nevertheless nonetheless by the same token similarly just as likewise in similar fashion as … as 💡MUST KNOW: Familiarize yourself with the common comparison signal words and be on the lookout for them. Let’s now discuss the important concept that comparisons must be logical.

item.title: 8.3 Comparisons Must be Logical
item.description: Lesson Content Whenever we make a comparison, that comparison must be logical. Let’s discuss how these illogical comparisons typically occur. When an Item Is Illogically Compared With a Different Type of Item Some illogical comparisons involve an item compared with a different type of item. Consider the following sentence: Incorrect: The cost of summer camp is much higher than summer school. Let’s identify the two items being compared. We can’t logically compare summer school with the cost of summer camp.

item.title: 8.4 When Something is Mistakenly Compared With Itself
item.description: Lesson Content Another type of illogical comparison involves the comparison of something with itself. Incorrect: Julie is more skilled at singing than any singer I’ve encountered. This sentence expresses a comparison of Julie with any singer that the speaker has ever encountered. Since Julie cannot be more skilled than herself, we need to distinguish between Julie and “any singer.” We can do this by inserting the word “other,” as demonstrated below:

item.title: 8.5 Verbs in Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content So far, whether examples that we have considered have been logical has depended primarily on whether comparing the things compared, which are named by nouns, makes sense. One thing that we can be sure of is that repeating in the second part of a comparison the verb or verb phrase that appears in the first part of a comparison is generally going to work. Jim attends the club meetings less regularly than Andrew attends the club meetings.

item.title: 8.6 Ellipsis in Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content While the word “ellipsis” may not be familiar to you, ellipsis is not a complex concept. Consider the following example: The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members. When we read the above sentence, we understand it to mean the following: The head of sales attends webinars more often than other team members attend webinars. In the first version, the wording “attend webinars” is omitted after “other team members.

item.title: 8.7 Ambiguous Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content As we have seen, omitting repeated words in a sentence that expresses a comparison is OK as long as the sentence still expresses its meaning effectively. Consider the following sentence: Incorrect: Alex is much nicer to me than Alfredo. This sentence could be interpreted in two different ways. In order for the sentence to be effective, the sentence has to include words that help clarify the meaning. To convey the first meaning unambiguously, we can say:

item.title: 8.8 Incomplete Comparisons
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes a sentence leaves a comparison incomplete. Let’s look at an example: Incorrect: This tent is just as waterproof. The meaning of this sentence is ambiguous because of an incomplete comparison. This tent is just as waterproof as more expensive models. The comparison becomes more complete via the addition of a noun to which the existing noun is compared. Incorrect: This project will cost less. Cost less than what?

item.title: 8.9 Sentences That Express Comparisons That Are Incomplete Even Though the Items Meant to Be Compared Are All Mentioned
item.description: Lesson Content There is another move involving incomplete comparisons that the GMAT can make. Incorrect: When it is compared with eating butter, eating olive oil is healthier. Notice what’s going on in the above example. Yes, the sentence says “compared with eating butter,” but “compared with eating butter” is part of a modifying clause, “when it is compared with eating butter,” that modifies “eating olive oil is healthier.” So, read literally, the sentence conveys that eating olive oil is healthier than something else – the sentence does not say what else – at certain times.

item.title: 8.10 “Like” versus “Such As” and “Including”
item.description: Lesson Content The word “like” can be used in a comparison, but not to introduce an example. To see the difference between the use of “like” and the use of “such as” demonstrated, consider the example sentences below: Incorrect: When traveling through the Everglades, one may see many animals, like snakes, mammals, and alligators. Since “like” is strictly a comparison word on the GMAT, using it in this context would imply that one may see, not the animals mentioned, but rather animals similar to, or “like,” snakes, mammals, or alligators.

item.title: 8.11 “Like” versus “As”
item.description: Lesson Content Just as people often misuse the words “like” and “such as,” they also often use the word “like” when the word “as” is needed. Incorrect: The teacher acts like she were a drill sergeant. While “as” is a versatile word that can be used as a preposition or a conjunction, the comparison word “like” can be used only as a preposition. So, the above example does not make sense, since, in the above example, the object of “like” is a clause, “she were a drill sergeant.

item.title: 8.12 Comparisons That Involve Quantity Words and Countable or Uncountable Nouns
item.description: Lesson Content Some nouns are countable, meaning that they have plural forms and that their plural forms name quantities made up of countable items. Other nouns are uncountable, meaning that quantities named by these nouns are not made up of countable items. One reason why understanding the distinction between countable and uncountable nouns is useful in GMAT Sentence Correction is that the words used in expressing comparisons involving quantities, called “quantity words,” vary along with the types of quantities compared.

item.title: 8.13 “Number” versus “Amount”
item.description: Lesson Content We use “number” with countable nouns and “amount” with uncountable nouns. For example: The number of species worldwide is declining. Notice that “species” is a countable noun. The number of people at the beach was amazing. Of course, “people” is a countable noun. The amount of enthusiasm on display at the candidate’s rally was shocking. “Enthusiasm” can’t be counted. The amount of water in the beaker is not sufficient for this experiment.

item.title: 8.14 Correctly Using Quantity Words When Discussing Quantities of Time, Money, or Weight
item.description: Lesson Content Time, money, and weight may seem to be countable. For instance, we know that we would use “less” with “water,” as does the following example: The tank currently contains less than two gallons of water. Money works similarly, as we can see in the following example: The account currently holds less than two dollars. Time works similarly, as we can see in the following example: The sun will set less than five hours from now.

item.title: 8.15 “Greater Than” versus “More Than”
item.description: Lesson Content “Greater than” is used in comparing the sizes, levels, degrees, magnitudes, or extents of things. “More … than” is not synonymous with “larger than” or “higher than.” “More” can be used in multiple ways, including as a determiner that directly modifies what follows it. Consider the following example: Her dislike of gambling is greater than her dislike of exercise. Notice that, in this case, the magnitudes of her dislikes are compared.

item.title: 8.16 Maintaining Awareness of Whether Comparison Expressions Use Correct Combinations of Words
item.description: Lesson Content As we have seen, to a large degree, GMAT Sentence Correction is a test of skill in noticing issues. Another way in which Sentence Correction questions test the same skills is through the use of incorrect combinations of words in comparison expressions. So, in order to ensure that we will correctly answer Sentence Correction questions involving comparisons, we have to maintain awareness of whether the comparison expressions in the sentences use correct combinations of words.

item.title: 8.17 “Whereas,” “While,” and “Although” Are Different From “Unlike.”
item.description: Lesson Content It’s common for people to perceive “whereas” and “unlike” as somewhat synonymous or as functioning in similar ways in sentences. Now, we’ve already learned that, when we see a comparison that employs “unlike,” the rules governing such a comparison are fairly strict and that the two elements related by “unlike” have to be rather similar. Unlike Stacy Ann, Jovan enjoys photography. Notice that two people named by proper nouns, “Stacy Ann” and “Jovan” are compared.

item.title: 8.18 What Appears to Be the Best Written, Clearest Comparison May Be in an Incorrect Choice.
item.description: Lesson Content As we have discussed previously, in order to force test-takers to use logic in choosing among Sentence Correction choices rather than simply choose a choice that “sounds correct,” Sentence Correction question writers often seek to make incorrect choices seem correct and correct choices seem incorrect. Consider the following two sentence versions: Version 1: Having been hunted to near extinction in the wild, there are now more tigers living in captivity in zoos, sanctuaries, and private hands than there are living in the wild.
